summary: DHH argues that Europe is suffering from a dangerous gap between its self-image and reality across multiple dimensions: free speech, tech competitiveness, energy policy, defense spending, and economic growth. He frames the EU's €120 million fine on X as emblematic of censorship rather than consumer protection, noting thousands of yearly arrests across the UK, Germany, and France for online speech. He highlights Europe's failure to produce major tech companies, its stagnating GDP relative to America's, and its dependency on others for energy and security. Despite the grim assessment, he concludes Europe isn't doomed if it pursues nuclear energy, merit-based immigration, deregulation, and drops its censorship apparatus.
key_points: The EU's fine on X is selective enforcement serving censorship goals, since Meta runs the same verification scheme without consequences
Europe's tech sector is so anemic that EU tech fines generated more revenue than all income taxes paid by European public internet companies in 2024
Germany hasn't grown real GDP in five years, partly due to energy costs 2-3x those of the US and China after spending €700 billion on green energy
The EU-US GDP gap has widened from near-parity in 2008 to America being 50% larger, with growth trajectories suggesting America's economy could be nearly twice Europe's within a decade
Europe retains enormous human capital potential but its policy environment drives talent emigration rather than retention
money_quotes: The gap between Europe's self-image and reality has grown into a chasm of delulu.
The EU's tech-fine operation produced more income for European coffers than all the income taxes paid by its public internet tech companies in 2024.
Europe basically stopped creating new, large companies more than half a century ago.

These should all be sobering numbers to any European. Whether it's the 10,000 yearly arrests in the UK for social media posts or the risk of an economy that's half the size of the American one in a decade.

There are plenty of reasons to prefer living in Europe, but that doesn't offset the fact that America is simply a vastly richer country, and that matters when it comes to everything from commercial dominance to military power.
There are plenty of paths to take that do not end in a low-growth, censorious regime that continues to export many of its best brains to America and elsewhere.
key_insight: Europe's compounding failures in free speech, tech competitiveness, energy policy, and defense spending are creating dangerous dependencies, but reversing course on regulation, energy, and immigration could still avert decline.
